cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out this question. The question is about what is implied regarding deep acting from the passage. First, I need to recall what deep acting is. From the passage, it's mentioned that "deep acting" is when people make themselves smile by thinking of pleasant memories. It's contrasted with "surface acting" which is just faking a smile without changing one's actual feelings. The question asks what is implied about deep acting. The options are: A) doesn't have any effect on men B) cannot improve our moods in any case C) harms our feelings in the long run D) pleases people by feeling like another person Let me check the relevant parts of the passage. The passage states: "Dr. Scott said, 'Women were harmed more by surface acting, meaning their moods worsened even more than men's. However, they were helped more by deep acting, which means their moods improved more by thinking of pleasant memories. '" So, deep acting helps women's moods improve more than men's. So it does have an effect on women, but what about men? The passage doesn't explicitly say if it affects men, but the fact that women were helped more by deep acting suggests that men were also helped, just less so. So option A, which says deep acting doesn't affect men, is incorrect. Option B says deep acting cannot improve moods in any case. But the passage clearly states that deep acting helps women's moods. So B is incorrect. Option C says deep acting harms feelings in the long run. The passage does mention that although deep acting can improve moods short-term, it's not a long-term solution. Dr. Scott says, "Although deep acting can improve moods a little in the short term, Dr. Scott says, it's not a long-term solution to feeling unhappy. 'There have been some suggestions that if you do this over a long period you start to feel unreal. You're trying to develop positive emotions, but at the end of the day you may not feel like yourself any more.'"
